channel.title: Jax Morning Brief
channel.description: Your daily rundown of national headlines, Jacksonville and Northeast Florida local news, home lending updates, and the latest in AI. Produced by Akilesh.
channel.itunes:author: Akilesh

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Warsh Takes Fed Gavel Into Selloff, Trump-Xi Summit Wraps Thin, 10Y at 4.59%
item.description: Kevin Warsh's first weekend as Fed chair opens with stocks tumbling and yields at a 15-month high. Trump wraps a Beijing summit short on deliverables. Google I/O kicks off Tuesday.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning, and welcome back from the weekend. It's Monday, May 18th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: A new Fed chair took the gavel Friday into a market that did not love what it saw. Kevin Warsh's first hours on the job came as the ten-year Treasury yield punched to a fifteen-month high and Wall Street sold off hard.

ANDREW: And President Trump wrapped two days in Beijing with Xi Jinping with a Boeing order, a soybean deal, and a vague handshake on Iran — but no joint statement. We'll get to what traders made of all of that, and what to watch this week.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed Friday at 7,408, down 1.24 percent. The Dow shed 537 points, or 1.07 percent, to finish at 49,526. The Nasdaq fell 1.54 percent to 26,225. It was the worst session in weeks, with tech leading the slide. The ten-year Treasury yield settled at 4.59 percent — the highest since February of last year — and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking at 6.36 percent according to Freddie Mac's weekly survey. The proximate cause was a messy week of inflation data, a new Fed chair, and a China summit that delivered atmospherics rather than deliverables.

ANDREW: A quick weekend at a glance. Warsh formally took the Fed chair from Jerome Powell Friday at noon. Trump and Xi closed their Beijing meetings the same morning. And on Sunday, the President said he will convene his national security team Tuesday to talk Iran options. We start there.

ANDREW: So Jenny, the big handoff. Friday at noon, Kevin Warsh became the eleventh chair of the Federal Reserve. Powell handed him the gavel after the slimmest confirmation margin for any Fed chair in modern history — 54 to 45 — and Warsh walks in with two problems on his desk. The first is a ten-year Treasury yield at 4.59 percent. The second is producer-price inflation that ran way hot the week before.

JENNY: And the market read on Warsh seems to be skeptical?

ANDREW: That's the right word. Bond traders spent the week pricing out rate cuts. Futures had a June cut as a coin flip ten days ago. After Friday they are well under thirty percent. Warsh has not said a word publicly since being sworn in, but during his confirmation hearing he told the Senate the Fed needs, quote, a new framework, new tools, and new communications. Markets are reading that as: don't expect easy cuts. His first FOMC meeting is June 16th and 17th.

JENNY: So whatever Warsh says first — wherever he says it — that's the print of the week?

ANDREW: Bigger than any data release this month. Powell, by the way, is staying on as a board governor. Which is unusual and worth watching.

ANDREW: The second weekend story — President Trump wrapped his Beijing summit with Xi Jinping on Friday. Trump called it, quote, fantastic trade deals. The actual deliverables: China committed to buying 200 Boeing aircraft, agreed to resume purchases of American soybeans, oil, and liquefied natural gas, and reopened access for U.S. beef. On Taiwan, the two sides agreed to disagree. There was no joint statement, which by summit standards is a real tell.

JENNY: And on Iran? That's what everyone was watching.

ANDREW: On Iran, Trump said Xi agreed not to send military equipment to Tehran and would, in his words, pressure Iran on the Strait of Hormuz. That is vague, and the markets read it as vague. By Sunday, Trump told reporters there, quote, will not be anything left of Iran if they don't move, and Axios is reporting he's meeting with the national security team Tuesday to weigh military options. Brent crude is back trading north of 107 dollars.

JENNY: A nine-week-old war that the White House still officially calls a ceasefire.

ANDREW: That's the surreal part. We are in day fifty-something of a ceasefire that has had two missile and drone attacks on the United Arab Emirates in the last week alone. Watch Tuesday's meeting closely.

ANDREW: That's the national desk. Jenny, over to home lending — though it's basically the same story, told through rate sheets.

JENNY: Andrew, the mortgage story this morning really is the same story. Walk us through where rates actually landed.

ANDREW: Sure. Freddie Mac's weekly survey out Thursday showed the thirty-year fixed at 6.36 percent. Bankrate's daily had it slightly higher around 6.43, and the top-tier rate at Mortgage News Daily came in closer to 6.5. So depending on which dashboard you read, the headline rate is somewhere between 6.36 and 6.5 percent. Either way, it is sitting at or near a five-week high, and the cause is the bond market — the ten-year jumped fourteen basis points Friday alone.

JENNY: So what does that actually mean for someone trying to close on a house this week?

ANDREW: Real money. On a 400,000 dollar loan, the difference between the early-April rate of about 6.2 and Friday's 6.5 is roughly 80 dollars a month — almost a thousand a year. For a buyer trying to qualify by debt-to-income, that's the difference between an approval and a no.

JENNY: And applications? Are buyers blinking?

ANDREW: Not yet, actually. The Mortgage Bankers Association reported purchase applications up four percent week-over-week for the week ending May 8th, and up seven percent year-over-year. Refis up 28 percent year-over-year. The market is still functional — it is just functional at a higher rate. The risk is what happens if the ten-year stays above 4.5 percent into June. That's when purchase volume actually starts to slow.

JENNY: And the FHA share?

ANDREW: Ticked up to 17.9 percent, which tells you first-time buyers are stretching. Watch the next MBA print Wednesday morning.

JENNY: Speaking of this week — let's pivot to AI. Andrew, the tech calendar is dominated by one event. Google I/O kicks off Tuesday in Mountain View, and Google is expected to put Gemini at the center of essentially everything they own.

ANDREW: Bigger than usual?

JENNY: Significantly. The keynote drops Tuesday at one p.m. Eastern. Expectations include a new Gemini model — possibly called Gemini 4.0 — agentic AI features baked into Android, the public preview of Android XR glasses built with Samsung and Warby Parker, and the unveiling of a new laptop category called Googlebooks from Acer, ASUS, and Lenovo. CNBC's framing is the sharpest: Google is racing to rewire Android around Gemini before Apple's AI reboot at the developer conference next month.

ANDREW: So this is a competitive shot, not just a developer conference.

JENNY: It's a shot at Apple, and a shot at OpenAI's super app play. OpenAI confirmed last week it's merging ChatGPT, the Codex coding agent, the developer API, and the Atlas browser into a single product team. So Google, OpenAI, and Apple are all converging on the same idea — your phone, your computer, and your browser should be one continuous agent.

JENNY: Meanwhile Anthropic had a remarkable week of its own. Bloomberg reports Anthropic is in advanced talks to raise as much as fifty billion dollars at a valuation between 850 billion and 900 billion. That would make it the most valuable AI startup in the world, leapfrogging OpenAI's 852 billion. CEO Dario Amodei told staff the company could grow 80 times this year.

ANDREW: 80 times. From what base?

JENNY: From a revenue base already in the billions. Worth keeping in perspective how fast this has happened — Anthropic was valued at 61 billion fourteen months ago. It is now flirting with a trillion. And separately, the company closed a 1.5 billion dollar joint venture with Blackstone, Goldman Sachs, Hellman and Friedman, and General Atlantic to push Claude into private-equity portfolio companies. Anthropic, Blackstone, and Hellman each put in roughly 300 million. Goldman about 150.

ANDREW: That's a financial services beachhead.

JENNY: It's the financial services beachhead. And one quick policy note — the federal Center for AI Standards and Innovation announced agreements this month with Google, Microsoft, and xAI to test their frontier models before public release. OpenAI and Anthropic renegotiated their existing partnerships to align with the Trump administration's AI Action Plan. Pre-release government testing of frontier models is now effectively table stakes.

JENNY: That's the AI desk. Let me bring it home to Jacksonville — and first the weather.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at 87 degrees and mostly sunny today, with a low around 70. Light easterly winds picking up to about 14 miles an hour in the afternoon. And the burn ban remains in effect across Duval County — smoke from wildfires in surrounding counties is keeping particulate levels elevated for sensitive groups.

JENNY: On the city front — Tuesday is the day for JEA. The City Council Finance Committee meets tomorrow and is expected to vote on whether to issue subpoenas to JEA CEO Vickie Cavey, Chief Administrative Officer Jody Brooks, and former chief legal counsel Regina Ross. Under council rules the Finance Committee can finalize the subpoena power without a full council vote. That makes Tuesday the decision day in a four-month-long workplace-culture and oversight fight.

ANDREW: And remind me — what's Cavey's response been?

JENNY: She's pushed back on the timing. In emails to council members earlier this month she said she could not appear before June 22nd, citing schedule conflicts and hurricane-season preparation. The committee is unlikely to be moved by that.

ANDREW: Story two?

JENNY: Story two is a big economic-development play. Council President Kevin Carrico filed Ordinance 2026-0419 last week — a 35 million dollar incentive package to land a Culinary Institute of America campus downtown. The Jax Daily Record has the financial breakdown: 8 million in workforce development funding, 27 million in other appropriations, paid out over six years. The campus would sit on the long-vacant waterfront site of the old county courthouse, inside a Corner Lot Development tower with a hotel and restaurants. Carrico is asking for emergency passage at the May 26th council meeting because the CIA board is meeting in June to pick its Southeast campus city.

ANDREW: Jacksonville against who?

JENNY: The city is not naming competitors publicly, but Atlanta, Charleston, and Nashville are the obvious ones. This would be the CIA's first new American campus in years.

JENNY: And the Jaguars released their 2026 schedule Thursday night. Browns at home Week 1, September 13th. Bye is Week 7, October 25th — and that placement is deliberate. The Jax Daily Record reports the team and city are using the bye to push stadium construction. Three prime-time games this year: at Baltimore November 5th on Amazon, home against Pittsburgh December 14th on ESPN Monday night, and at Dallas December 27th on NBC. Two London games early — Eagles October 11th, Texans October 18th.

ANDREW: So a brutal travel stretch up front and then a working-class Sunday-afternoon schedule.

JENNY: Pretty much.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this week: Tuesday has the loaded calendar. Trump's national security meeting on Iran. The Google I/O keynote at one p.m. Eastern. The JEA Finance Committee vote in Jacksonville. And in the background, every bond trader on Wall Street is going to be listening for the first public sound from Fed Chair Kevin Warsh. The signal to watch: whether the ten-year Treasury yield closes above 4.6 percent. That is the threshold where economists at Goldman and JPMorgan have said mortgage rates start moving meaningfully toward 6.75 percent — and where the housing market begins to feel it.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Monday. Have a great week.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Warsh Sworn In, Trump Lands in Beijing, Mortgage Rates Hit 5-Week High
item.description: Kevin Warsh confirmed as Fed Chair in the closest vote in modern history. Trump and Xi open a two-day summit in Beijing. A hot producer prices print pushes the 10-year Treasury to a 10-month high.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Thursday, May 14th. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: Two enormous stories driving the day. Kevin Warsh was confirmed last night as the next chair of the Federal Reserve, in the closest vote for a Fed chair in modern history.

ANDREW: And President Trump landed in Beijing overnight for a two-day summit with Xi Jinping — the first state visit to China by a sitting U.S. president since 2017. Xi opened by asking the President, on camera, whether the two countries could avoid what he called the Thucydides Trap.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed Wednesday at 7,444 — a fresh record — up six tenths of a percent. The Nasdaq also hit a record, finishing at 26,402, up 1.2%. The Dow slipped 67 points to 49,693. It was a split tape. Tech rallied hard. The broader market was weaker. The ten-year Treasury yield finished the day at 4.47% — its highest level since July. And the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking near 6.57% this morning, according to Mortgage News Daily — the highest reading in about five weeks. The driver was a hot producer prices report. PPI came in at 1.4% for the month, the largest monthly jump since March of 2022, and well above the 0.5% consensus.

ANDREW: Let's start at the Fed. Last night the Senate confirmed Kevin Warsh as the eleventh chair of the Federal Reserve, 54 to 45. That is the slimmest confirmation margin for a Fed chair in the modern era. Only one Democrat — Pennsylvania's John Fetterman — crossed over. Warsh, who's 56, served on the Fed board from 2006 to 2011. He takes the gavel from Jerome Powell on Friday. His first FOMC meeting as chair is June 16th and 17th.

JENNY: Andrew, walk me through what this actually changes. Is the market expecting faster rate cuts because of who Warsh is — or in spite of him?

ANDREW: Fair question. President Trump has been explicit that he wants deeper, faster cuts, and he picked Warsh expecting cooperation. But Warsh himself spent the back half of his last Fed tenure arguing the central bank kept money too easy for too long after the financial crisis. So the bond market is hedging. Yesterday's hot PPI print actually pushed cut expectations further out, not closer. Futures are now pricing roughly a coin flip for a June cut.

JENNY: And forty-five no votes — that's the institutional cost.

ANDREW: It is. This is the most polarized handoff the Fed has ever seen. Watch Warsh's first public remarks. The tone he sets in his first week matters more for the ten-year yield than any data point this month. If he sounds like Powell, the market relaxes. If he sounds like he's auditioning for the White House, the long end of the curve gets ugly fast.

ANDREW: Story two on the national desk. The President is in Beijing this morning. He and Xi Jinping opened a two-day summit a few hours ago. Per CNBC reporting from the ground, Xi opened the talks by asking Trump on camera whether the U.S. and China can avoid the Thucydides Trap — the idea that a rising power and an established power are destined for conflict. That is unusually direct language for a Chinese leader to use with a U.S. president on day one.

JENNY: What's actually on the table — concretely?

ANDREW: Four buckets. First, extending the fragile trade truce from last year. Second, rare earths access, which matters for U.S. semiconductors and defense supply chains. Third, the Iran war — Beijing has real leverage on Tehran and Washington wants help reopening the Strait of Hormuz. And fourth, an AI safety framework — that's a longer-term play, but the language they land on will set the template for the next two years. Boeing is also expected to come up. There's been speculation about a large Chinese aircraft order as part of the trade package. The two leaders meet again through midday Friday.

JENNY: And on Iran specifically — where do things stand this morning?

ANDREW: Still on what the President called massive life support earlier this week. Brent crude settled near 107 dollars yesterday, WTI just above 101. Hormuz traffic remains well below pre-war levels. If the summit produces even a vague Chinese commitment to pressure Tehran on reopening the strait, that is the kind of headline that pulls crude down five to seven dollars in a session — and pulls the ten-year yield with it.

JENNY: And the stakes if it stays closed?

ANDREW: The CEO of Saudi Aramco said earlier this week that if Hormuz remains closed past mid-June, the global oil market doesn't normalize until 2027. Cumulative supply losses from the closure have already exceeded one billion barrels. So this is not a marginal story for inflation or for the rate path — it is the story. Every other macro number this month is downstream of it.

ANDREW: Jenny, that's the national desk. Over to you for Jacksonville.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high of 85 degrees today, sunny, with an overnight low near 61. A welcome dry stretch after two days of soaking rain earlier this week — though the longer-term drought deficit still sits at more than 25 inches.

JENNY: Our top local story stays on JEA. The city council's investigation into workplace culture at the utility moved forward this week. The special committee voted Monday to seek subpoenas for CEO Vickie Cavey, Chief Administrative Officer Jody Brooks, and former chief legal counsel Regina Ross. The full council could vote on authorizing those subpoenas as early as May 19th. Separately, council approved a 9,250 dollar contract Tuesday night with a California firm called SelectionLink to survey current and former JEA employees on workplace culture.

ANDREW: What's Cavey's response been?

JENNY: She's pushed back on the timing. In emails to council, she said her schedule and hurricane season preparation make it impossible to appear before June 22nd. So even if the subpoenas get authorized next week, expect a procedural fight on the calendar before any testimony happens.

JENNY: One more local item before the Jaguars news. The city is moving forward on a 35 million dollar incentive package to land a Culinary Institute of America campus in Jacksonville. The Jax Daily Record reported earlier this week that the ordinance has been introduced. It had been moving under the code name Project Caymus. The full committee referral and the first public hearing are the next steps to watch — along with CIA itself confirming the deal publicly.

ANDREW: That's a serious anchor for downtown if it lands.

JENNY: It is. A culinary school of that profile would seed a real restaurant and hospitality ecosystem around it — well beyond the campus footprint.

JENNY: And then tonight — the Jaguars' full 2026 schedule drops at 7:30 Eastern. Opponents are already known. Both London games are confirmed: the Eagles at Tottenham Hotspur Stadium on October 11th, the Texans at Wembley a week later on October 18th. What we get tonight is the order of games, the prime-time count, the bye week, and the Week One opponent.

ANDREW: And the Winn-Dixie incentive package — that finally closed this week?

JENNY: Approved 16 to 2 on Tuesday night. Twelve million dollars over five and twenty years, in exchange for 65 million in capital investment from the company, 500 retained jobs, 200 new jobs at around 100K average salary, and a commitment to keep the Brentwood Harveys open through at least February of 2029. That last piece is now written into the ordinance — which is what finally got the deal across after it stalled in committee earlier this month.

JENNY: Andrew, what's happening on the mortgage side?

ANDREW: Rates moved higher again. Per Mortgage News Daily, the top-tier average thirty-year fixed jumped to 6.57% on Wednesday — the highest reading in roughly five weeks. Optimal Blue's daily average sits at 6.40%. And the MBA's weekly survey, which lags a few days, has the conforming average at 6.46% for the week ending May 8th.

JENNY: So what's pushing this — Warsh, or the inflation data?

ANDREW: Mostly the data. Producer prices ran 1.4% for the month — the largest monthly gain in four years. That pushed the ten-year yield to 4.47%, its highest since July. Mortgage rates trade directly off that ten-year, so the move was almost mechanical. The Warsh confirmation is layered on top — it adds some uncertainty premium — but the bond market had largely priced him in already.

JENNY: Got it.

ANDREW: Now the surprise. Despite higher rates, application volume actually rose. The MBA reported total applications up 1.7% for the week ending May 8th. Purchase applications climbed 4% week-over-week, and they are running 7% ahead of the same week a year ago. Refinance dipped 1% — but is still up 28% year-over-year.

JENNY: That's counterintuitive. Rates up, applications up?

ANDREW: A pattern we've been seeing all spring. Buyers who pre-approved in March are locking in rather than waiting, because they expect any cuts later this year to take months to flow through. And the FHA share keeps creeping higher — now 17.9% of total applications. That tells you more first-time and credit-constrained buyers are in the mix. It's an affordability story, not a rate story.

ANDREW: Jenny, AI desk is yours.

JENNY: For the first time, Anthropic has more paying business customers than OpenAI. According to the May AI Index from the fintech firm Ramp, 34.4% of participating businesses are paying for Anthropic services. OpenAI sits at 32.3%. Twelve months ago Anthropic's share was 9%. OpenAI's share over that same period actually slipped about a point.

ANDREW: How is Ramp measuring this?

JENNY: They track corporate card spending across their customer base. So this is verified dollars leaving the bank — not a survey. It's the cleanest public read we have on actual enterprise adoption. And it puts Anthropic ahead in the segment that matters most for revenue durability.

JENNY: The other big AI story — Anthropic's fundraising. The company is in advanced talks for a round between 30 and 50 billion dollars at a valuation as high as 950 billion. If it closes at the top of that range, Anthropic becomes the most valuable private AI company in the world, ahead of OpenAI. Bloomberg has reported the company is also weighing an IPO as soon as October.

ANDREW: And on the OpenAI side?

JENNY: OpenAI launched a consulting arm Monday — they're calling it the OpenAI Deployment Company — with four billion dollars in initial investment. They're also acquiring an applied AI firm called Tomoro. The strategic read is that both labs are racing to lock in enterprise relationships before the gap between frontier models gets any narrower. The model is no longer the moat. The integration is.

ANDREW: And the cybersecurity race between the two — any update on that this week?

JENNY: Still escalating. OpenAI's Daybreak initiative — built on GPT-5.5 and Codex to find and patch software vulnerabilities — launched earlier this week as a direct response to Anthropic's Project Glasswing, which already counts AWS, Microsoft, Google, JPMorgan Chase, and Palo Alto Networks as partners. Google warned over the weekend that it had caught hackers using AI for live exploit development. So this isn't theoretical. It's already a defense-versus-offense arms race playing out in production.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today. The Trump-Xi summit deliverables out of Beijing this afternoon and tomorrow morning. Specifically, watch two things. First, any joint language on the Strait of Hormuz — even a vague Chinese commitment to pressure Tehran on reopening it would move oil five-plus dollars and pull the ten-year yield with it. Second, a Boeing order announcement. If either materializes, expect Boeing shares to gap higher on Friday's open and crude to soften. Those two headlines will set the tone for next week's rate path.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Thursday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Hot CPI Lifts Rates, Warsh Faces Final Vote, Winn-Dixie Deal Passes
item.description: April inflation hit 3.8 percent, the hottest in nearly three years, sending mortgage rates higher just as the Senate prepares its final vote on Kevin Warsh for Fed chair. Plus Jacksonville approves the Winn-Dixie deal.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Wednesday, May 13th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: April inflation came in hotter than economists expected, the hottest reading in almost three years, and the bond market did not take it well. We'll walk you through what that means for your mortgage rate this morning.

ANDREW: And the Senate is expected to take its final vote today confirming Kevin Warsh as the next chair of the Federal Reserve, just two days before Jerome Powell's term ends.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 slipped 0.16 percent to close at 7,400.96. The Dow eked out a small gain, up 56 points or about a tenth of a percent, ending at 49,760.56. The Nasdaq took the worst of it, down 0.71 percent to 26,088.20. The ten-year Treasury yield jumped roughly 5 basis points to 4.46 percent on the inflation print, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking near 6.4 percent this morning according to Bankrate, up from about 6.19 percent before the data hit. The selling was concentrated in tech and rate-sensitive names — a textbook hot-CPI reaction.

ANDREW: Let's start there, because everything else this morning runs through it. The Bureau of Labor Statistics reported April CPI rose 0.6 percent for the month, putting the annual headline rate at 3.8 percent. That's the hottest reading since May 2023, and it came in a tenth of a percent above the Dow Jones consensus. Core inflation, which strips out food and energy, rose 0.4 percent for the month and 2.8 percent over the year — also above forecast.

JENNY: So what's actually driving that? Is this the Iran story finally showing up in the data?

ANDREW: Largely, yes. Energy prices jumped 3.8 percent in a single month and accounted for more than 40 percent of the headline gain. Gasoline alone is up 28 percent over the year. That's the Hormuz blockade and the war risk premium working its way to the pump. But — and this is the part that worries the Fed more — shelter costs reaccelerated to 0.6 percent after several months of easing, and tariff-sensitive categories like apparel and airline fares are running hot. Airline fares are now up almost 21 percent year over year.

JENNY: Real wages?

ANDREW: Negative. Average hourly wages slipped half a percent for the month and are down 0.3 percent over the year in real terms. Paychecks are losing ground to prices for the first time in roughly two years.

ANDREW: Which brings us to Kevin Warsh. The Senate confirmed Warsh to a seat on the Federal Reserve Board last night by a 51-to-45 vote, almost entirely along party lines — Pennsylvania Democrat John Fetterman the only crossover. That was the easier vote. The separate vote to confirm him as chair is expected today, and Republican leadership has lined up the 30-hour clock to get it done before Powell's term as chair ends Friday.

JENNY: Walk us through why this matters today specifically, and not just in June.

ANDREW: Two reasons. First, Warsh inherits an inflation backdrop that just got materially worse, and the market wants to know on day one whether he's the hawk his record suggests or whether he'll defer to the data flow. Second, the Fed's next policy meeting is June 16th and 17th. Before yesterday's CPI, futures markets were roughly a coin flip on a June rate cut. After the print, the probability dropped sharply. Warsh's first public remarks as chair-designate will essentially set the tone for that meeting.

JENNY: And Powell himself — he's not gone Friday, right?

ANDREW: Correct. His term as chair ends Friday, but he remains a member of the Board of Governors and can stay on until 2028 if he wants. Whether he does is one of the open questions of the week.

ANDREW: The other big-picture national story is Iran. President Trump rejected Iran's latest ceasefire counter-proposal on Monday, called it, quote, a piece of garbage, and said the ceasefire is, quote, on massive life support. Shipping traffic in the Strait of Hormuz remained frozen yesterday. Brent crude settled at 107.77 dollars a barrel, up 3.4 percent. WTI closed above 102. The Saudi Aramco CEO warned this week that if Hormuz stays closed past mid-June, the oil market won't normalize until 2027.

JENNY: Which feeds right back into the CPI conversation we just had.

ANDREW: Exactly. The energy spike isn't an isolated shock anymore. It's becoming a persistent input into the inflation data, into rate expectations, into mortgage pricing — all of it.

JENNY: Speaking of mortgage pricing, that's a hard transition to Jenny, but here we are. Andrew, what's the rate move look like up close?

ANDREW: It's ugly. Mortgage News Daily had the thirty-year conforming rate at 6.19 percent Monday afternoon. Bankrate's daily average this morning is tracking near 6.45 percent. That's roughly a quarter-point move on a single inflation print, which is a very large one-day reaction.

JENNY: So if you locked your rate Monday morning, you got lucky. If you were planning to lock today —

ANDREW: You're paying real money for the delay. On a 400,000 dollar loan, a quarter-point move adds about 65 dollars to the monthly payment. Over the life of a thirty-year loan, you're talking roughly 24,000 dollars.

ANDREW: The Mortgage Bankers Association's latest weekly survey, for the week ending May 1st, showed applications down 4.4 percent overall, refinances down 5 percent. That data was collected before yesterday's CPI, so the next reading is likely to look worse. One detail from the MBA report worth noting — the FHA share of applications crept up again to 17.7 percent. That's a quiet signal that the buyer pool is increasingly tilted toward first-time and lower-down-payment borrowers, even as headline affordability gets worse.

JENNY: And on the regulatory side, anything moving this week?

ANDREW: Quiet on the regulatory front. The bigger near-term swing factor for rates is the Warsh tone, full stop. If he sounds hawkish in his first statement as chair, the ten-year yield could push toward 4.55 percent, and the thirty-year mortgage with it. If he signals data dependence, we may give some of yesterday's move back.

ANDREW: Jenny, over to you for Jacksonville.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high near 75 today with showers and a chance of thunderstorms — 90 percent chance of rain, with another half to three-quarters of an inch expected. That's day two of the soaker we've been waiting on for months, and it is doing real work on the drought deficit, though we're still down more than two feet of rainfall over the longer term.

JENNY: To City Hall. The Winn-Dixie incentive package passed last night, 16 to 2. The council approved a 12 million dollar package — that breaks down to a 6.5 million dollar headquarters retention grant paid out over five years, plus up to 5.5 million in property tax rebates over twenty years. In exchange, Winn-Dixie commits to a 65 million dollar capital investment, retains 500 existing headquarters jobs, adds 200 new jobs at an average salary of 100,000 dollars by 2031, and — this is the part that almost derailed it — keeps the Harveys Supermarket in Brentwood open through at least February 2029.

ANDREW: The two no votes?

JENNY: Council President Kevin Carrico and Councilman Rory Diamond. Most of the floor debate centered on the food-desert question — whether eighteen months of guaranteed Harveys operation was enough for a neighborhood that has limited grocery options. The final deal stretched that commitment to roughly three years and put it in the ordinance language rather than a handshake, which is what got Carrico's earlier objections mostly addressed but, ultimately, not his vote.

JENNY: Separately, the council took another step on the JEA investigation. Members approved a 9,250 dollar contract with a firm called SelectionLink to survey current and former JEA employees about workplace culture. That's tied to ongoing accusations of a toxic environment under CEO Vickie Cavey. And on Monday, the special committee voted to seek subpoenas for Cavey, the chief administrative officer, and the former chief legal counsel. The full council could vote on authorizing those subpoenas as soon as May 19th.

ANDREW: That's a notable escalation.

JENNY: It is. And one more from the Jax Daily Record yesterday — the city introduced an ordinance to explore a 35 million dollar incentive package to bring a Culinary Institute of America campus to Jacksonville. The project had been moving under the code name Project Caymus. No vote yet; this is the opening of the formal evaluation.

JENNY: Andrew, anything from the Jaguars side worth a flag?

ANDREW: One date for the calendar. The NFL releases the full 2026 schedule tomorrow night at 7:30 Eastern. The Jaguars' opponents are already set — both AFC North and NFC East divisions, plus the AFC South rivals home and away — but the order, the prime-time count, and the bye week are what fans are waiting on.

JENNY: All right, let's pivot. The AI desk has two stories worth your time this morning.

JENNY: First — OpenAI launched a new initiative yesterday called Daybreak. It's an AI cybersecurity effort built on GPT-5.5 and the Codex coding model, and the framing is explicitly competitive. Daybreak is designed to find vulnerabilities, prioritize them, and then generate and test patches directly inside code repositories. The company says it reduces analyst review time from hours to minutes. The competitive context here is clear — Anthropic announced its Project Glasswing partnership last month with AWS, Microsoft, Google, JPMorgan Chase and others, and gated its offensive-capability Mythos model to a small group of approved organizations. Daybreak is OpenAI's answer.

ANDREW: Is this primarily an enterprise sales pitch or a genuine capability claim?

JENNY: Both, honestly. The enterprise pitch is real — large security operations centers are the most attractive AI customer right now because they have the budget, the labor cost pressure, and a clearly measurable workflow. But the underlying capability — finding and patching real-world vulnerabilities — is also where the labs are pushing hardest. Google issued a public warning Monday that it has caught hackers using AI to do exploit development in the wild. So the offensive-defensive race is genuinely on, and the frontier labs are positioning themselves as the defenders of choice.

JENNY: Second story — Anthropic is in advanced talks to acquire a developer tools company called Stainless. Reported price tag, at least 300 million dollars, per The Information. Stainless builds the software development kits that let companies plug AI models into their existing code — and notably, OpenAI and Google are among its current customers.

ANDREW: So Anthropic would be buying a piece of its competitors' developer infrastructure.

JENNY: Right. It's the kind of acquisition that doesn't make a flashy headline but matters a lot for how enterprise AI gets deployed. It also lands in the middle of Anthropic's reported 50 billion dollar fundraising round at a 900 billion dollar valuation. Acquisitions, hiring, and capacity build-out — that's where that money is going.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today: the Senate vote on Kevin Warsh as Fed chair. The vote is expected sometime in the afternoon. The number to watch is the bond market reaction in the hour after it lands — specifically, the ten-year Treasury yield. If it pushes through 4.5 percent, expect mortgage rates to follow toward 6.5 percent by the end of the week. If yields hold or drift lower, yesterday's CPI shock may have already done the worst of the damage.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Wednesday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Warsh Clears Cloture, Iran on Life Support, April CPI Drops at 8:30
item.description: The Senate advances Kevin Warsh as Powell heads for the door Friday. Trump calls the Iran ceasefire "on life support." April CPI lands this morning, and Jacksonville's Winn-Dixie vote returns tonight with the Brentwood store back in the deal.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Tuesday, May 12th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: Kevin Warsh cleared his cloture vote in the Senate last night, putting him three days from inheriting the Federal Reserve from Jay Powell — and the April CPI print drops in about an hour.

ANDREW: And the Iran ceasefire is, in the President's own words, on life support — while right here in Jacksonville, the Winn-Dixie deal returns for a full council vote tonight with a very different store-closure picture than a week ago.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed at 7,412 yesterday, up about two-tenths of a percent — its first close above 7,400 in history. The Dow added 95 points to finish at 49,704, also up two-tenths. The Nasdaq inched up about a tenth to 26,274. Both the S and P and the Nasdaq set new records. The ten-year Treasury yield is sitting at roughly 4.39 percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking around 6.19 percent this morning according to Mortgage News Daily. A quietly bullish session, but the real move is probably waiting for the 8:30 CPI print.

JENNY: That's a lot pinned to one number. Andrew, take it from the top — over to you for national.

ANDREW: Let's start with the Fed. The Senate voted 49 to 44 last night to invoke cloture on Kevin Warsh's nomination, clearing the procedural hurdle on his path to replacing Jay Powell as Federal Reserve chair. Roll Call is reporting Warsh still needs a separate up-or-down vote on the chair role specifically, but Republican leadership says they expect to finish both votes this week. Powell's term as chair ends Friday, May 15th — although he's confirmed he'll remain on the Fed's board for a period of time after stepping down.

JENNY: So this really does happen by Friday.

ANDREW: It really does. And the market read is already in motion. Warsh has spent months signaling a less interventionist Fed and a higher tolerance for letting market mechanisms work — which is code for cutting more aggressively if growth softens, and refusing to lean against fiscal policy. Traders are pricing in roughly a coin-flip on a June rate cut. The bigger question is whether his confirmation tone shifts the June FOMC meeting.

JENNY: And that brings us right to 8:30 this morning.

ANDREW: It does. The Bureau of Labor Statistics drops the April Consumer Price Index at 8:30 Eastern. Bloomberg consensus is 3.7 percent on the headline, year over year, up from 3.3 percent in March, and 2.7 percent on core. Two wrinkles. One — the oil-price spike from the Iran war should push the headline number higher. Two, and this one matters more, BLS is rolling in a delayed adjustment on owners' equivalent rent that was held flat back in October during the government shutdown. So expect noise in the core number that has nothing to do with the underlying inflation trend.

JENNY: How are desks set up?

ANDREW: The pain trade for the bond market is anything above 3.8 on headline or 3.0 on core. That puts the June rate-cut conversation in real trouble before Warsh even sits down. Anything cooler than expected, and the ten-year — and the thirty-year mortgage along with it — has room to grind lower into the summer.

ANDREW: Speaking of the war driving that oil spike — President Trump told reporters in the Oval Office yesterday that the U.S.-Iran ceasefire is, quote, on life support. He rejected Iran's counter-proposal Sunday night, calling it, quote, a piece of garbage. CNN and Bloomberg are both reporting that some Trump advisers say he is now seriously considering a return to major combat operations. Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remained effectively frozen yesterday.

JENNY: And the President leaves for Beijing on Thursday with all of that still open.

ANDREW: He does. Trump and Xi meet Thursday and Friday — the first state visit to China by a sitting U.S. President since Trump's own 2017 trip. CSIS is calling the summit "modest in ambition." Iran is expected to dominate the agenda, given how much Iranian oil China has historically imported. Watch for a Boeing aircraft purchase announcement and language on rare-earth access — those are the realistic deliverables. A comprehensive tariff deal is not on the table.

JENNY: Andrew, while we're on rates — what's the picture on home lending this morning?

ANDREW: Mortgage rates are basically holding their breath alongside the rest of the bond market. Mortgage News Daily has the thirty-year conforming average at 6.19 percent this morning, down six basis points from yesterday. Freddie Mac's weekly survey, which lags, came in at 6.37 percent last week, up from 6.30 percent the week before. The Mortgage Bankers Association reported overall applications down 4.4 percent for the latest week, with the refinance index off 5 percent — but here's the part that caught my eye — the average purchase loan size hit a record $467,300.

JENNY: A record purchase loan size in a 4.4 percent down week. What does that tell us?

ANDREW: It tells us who's left in the market. The marginal buyer right now is the higher-income, less rate-sensitive buyer — the move-up family with substantial equity, the retiree relocation, the high-net-worth second home. First-time and starter-home demand has been hollowed out by affordability for the better part of a year. The MBA composition data backs that up — the FHA share is creeping up, which usually means the lower-income buyers who are still in the market are stretching to the most leveraged products available to them.

JENNY: So a rate cut doesn't fix that quickly.

ANDREW: It doesn't fix it, no. But it does change the math at the margin. Desks I talk to are modeling that if Warsh is confirmed Thursday or Friday and strikes a dovish tone in his first remarks, we could see 5 to 10 basis points off the ten-year and roughly the same on the thirty-year. That's not a refi-wave-triggering move, but it would matter for anyone trying to lock this week.

ANDREW: One more thing on the regulatory side — the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau continues to operate at reduced staffing under the administration's restructuring, and HousingWire is reporting that lenders are watching for any rule changes coming out of FHFA on conforming loan limits later this quarter. Quiet on that front this morning, but it is a story that matters in the back half of the year.

JENNY: Got it. Let me take us to AI — there were two real developments yesterday that I think tell a bigger story together.

JENNY: First, Google issued what it's calling a dire warning yesterday, saying it has caught hackers using AI to break into computer systems. Fortune is reporting that Google's threat intelligence team has documented real-world deployments of AI tools to discover and exploit software vulnerabilities. And that comes the same week that Anthropic is gating access to its new Mythos model — which Anthropic itself says is so strikingly capable at offensive cybersecurity work that the company won't release it publicly, even to paying customers, beyond a small group of approved organizations.

ANDREW: So we're now in a world where the labs are publicly saying their own models are too dangerous to ship.

JENNY: Exactly. And on the defense side, Anthropic's Project Glasswing — launched in April — has been quietly expanding. The launch partners include AWS, Apple, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google, JPMorgan Chase, the Linux Foundation, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Palo Alto Networks. Anthropic is putting up to $100 million in usage credits and $4 million in donations behind it. The framing is, essentially, we built the weapon, here is the consortium to build the shield.

ANDREW: And how is OpenAI playing this?

JENNY: Differently. CNBC is reporting that OpenAI announced yesterday it will give the European Union early access to a variant of its newest model called GPT-5.5-Cyber. That's the same capability lane Anthropic is gating. OpenAI is choosing transparency through government access where Anthropic is choosing restriction. Both are defensible — but it means enterprise buyers are now picking vendors on AI policy posture, not just capability.

ANDREW: Which is exactly the conversation our lending and banking clients are having this quarter.

JENNY: It is. One quick aside on Anthropic itself — the Financial Times and TechCrunch are both reporting that the company's $50 billion funding round at a $900 billion valuation is in advanced stages. Anthropic asked investors to submit allocations within 48 hours. A board decision is expected this month. Revenue run rate is reported at $44 billion as of early May, up from roughly $9 billion at the end of 2025.

ANDREW: 9 billion to 44 billion in five months. That's a number that makes you do a double take regardless of where you sit on AI.

JENNY: It is. Andrew, let me bring us home to Jacksonville.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high of 75 degrees today with rain in the forecast. The National Weather Service has the chance of precipitation at 90 percent, with a half to three-quarters of an inch possible, and the possibility of thunderstorms this afternoon. After more than 25 inches of cumulative rainfall deficit, this is the first meaningful soaking the city has seen in months. The burn ban remains in place, but this is the start of the break we've been waiting for.

JENNY: City Council takes up the Winn-Dixie incentive package tonight at 5 p.m. The Jax Daily Record is reporting that Council leadership and Winn-Dixie have reached an agreement that keeps the Harveys Supermarket on West 48th Street open through February 2029, when its lease is set to expire. That Harveys closure was the sticking point that derailed the Finance Committee vote a week ago — residents argued the closure would create a food desert in Northwest Jacksonville.

ANDREW: So the 12 million is back on the table.

JENNY: It is. To recap — up to $12 million in incentives, made up of a $6.5 million headquarters retention grant paid out over five years and roughly $5.5 million in city property tax rebates over twenty years. In return, Winn-Dixie keeps its 500 existing headquarters jobs at Edgewood Court, adds 200 more by the end of 2031 at an average salary of at least $100,000, and commits $65 million in improvements — about $17 million at the headquarters and $48 million across its Jacksonville stores. First Coast News is reporting the Brentwood-store commitment is now baked into the ordinance language, not a verbal handshake.

ANDREW: That's a real shift from where this was last Tuesday.

JENNY: It is. And the politics matter — this is the kind of deal that signals to other corporate headquarters in the region that Council will hold a hard line on community-impact concessions, but will close the deal when the company shows up at the table.

ANDREW: And on the Jaguars front?

JENNY: One quick note. The NFL releases the full 2026 schedule Thursday night at 7:30 Eastern. The Jaguars already know their opponents, but not the order. The team plays eight home games at EverBank Stadium, with capacity dropped to 42,507 while the Stadium of the Future is under construction. There are two London games back-to-back, and the Pittsburgh game has been moved to Orlando. Construction of the new stadium targets August 2028 completion, which means the team plays the entire 2027 season on the road.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today: the April Consumer Price Index, released by the Bureau of Labor Statistics at 8:30 Eastern. Consensus is 3.7 percent on the headline, year over year, and 2.7 percent on core. The housing piece will swing the core number because of last fall's owners' equivalent rent adjustment getting backfilled, so expect noise. The clean read to watch: anything north of 3.8 on headline or 3.0 on core, and the June rate-cut conversation gets a lot harder for Kevin Warsh on what may literally be his first day on the job. Anything cooler, and the ten-year — and the thirty-year mortgage with it — has real room to move.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Tuesday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Trump Rejects Iran Reply, Warsh Cloture Vote Tonight, Anthropic Eyes $900B
item.description: Trump calls Iran's ceasefire response "totally unacceptable" as drones strike the Gulf. The Senate's first vote on Kevin Warsh as Fed chair is tonight. Anthropic eyes a $900 billion valuation.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning, and welcome back from the weekend. It's Monday, May eleventh, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: Iran's long-awaited response to the ceasefire proposal landed Sunday night, and President Trump rejected it within hours, calling it, in his words, totally unacceptable. The strait is back on a knife's edge as Wall Street opens this week.

ANDREW: And the Senate holds its first real vote on Kevin Warsh as Fed chair tonight at five thirty Eastern, with Powell's term running out on Friday. We'll get to what that means for mortgage rates.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed Friday at 7,399, up about eight tenths of a percent and another record. The Dow finished essentially flat at 49,609, up just 12 points. The Nasdaq jumped 1.7 percent to 26,247, also a fresh high. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at 4.38 percent. And the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking around 6.33 percent according to Optimal Blue, with Freddie Mac's weekly survey at 6.37. The big mover Friday was the April jobs print, 115,000 payrolls, roughly double what economists expected, and that's what nudged yields higher into the weekend.

ANDREW: Weekend at a glance. Iran delivered its long-awaited counter-proposal via Pakistan on Sunday, and President Trump rejected it on social media within hours. Drones hit Kuwait and the United Arab Emirates Friday and Saturday. And Anthropic is reportedly closing in on a 900 billion dollar valuation. We'll take them in order.

ANDREW: Let's start with Iran, because everything else this week reads through that story. Iran's foreign ministry delivered its response to the one-page U.S. memorandum on Sunday via Pakistani mediators. The response was not a yes. According to Iran's Tasnim news agency, Tehran is now demanding an end to the war on all fronts, and that explicitly includes Lebanon, where Israel is still fighting Hezbollah. Iran also wants the U.S. blockade on Iranian ports lifted, sanctions on Iranian oil sales removed, and frozen assets unfrozen. Trump posted to social media late Sunday that the response was, quote, totally unacceptable, in capital letters.

JENNY: So Andrew, what is the practical risk this week? Does this go hot again?

ANDREW: That's the question oil traders are wrestling with at the open this morning. Two things to watch. First, Trump warned last week that if no deal was reached, strikes would resume at, his words, a much higher level and intensity. That language is now activated. Second, over the weekend drones targeted Kuwait and the United Arab Emirates, both U.S. allies, and a commercial cargo ship caught fire in the Gulf. Iran's Revolutionary Guard had already warned of a heavy assault on Saturday. None of that means strikes happen today, but the ceasefire is functionally suspended until either Iran moderates its position or the U.S. moves militarily. Crude futures are the proxy to watch this week.

JENNY: And just to remind listeners, the Strait of Hormuz carries roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne oil. The whole point of Trump's one-page memorandum was to lock in reopening the strait first and push the harder nuclear questions into a 30-day negotiating window. Iran's counter is essentially: we'll talk about Hormuz, but only if you also pull back from Lebanon and unwind the entire sanctions architecture. That's a much bigger ask, and it's the gap Trump rejected last night.

ANDREW: Exactly right. And the maritime piece is why this matters even if the shooting stays limited. Insurance premiums on Gulf shipping are already up sharply since the skirmish last Thursday off Qeshm Island. Every additional drone strike, every fast-boat incident, ripples through tanker rates, then into refined product prices, then into the C-P-I print in two months. The Fed knows that. Warsh knows that. It's part of why tonight's vote matters.

JENNY: And this lands right before Trump's trip to Beijing.

ANDREW: It does. President Trump leaves for China Thursday for a two-day summit with Xi Jinping on the fourteenth and fifteenth. Iran complicates that meeting because Beijing buys the majority of Iranian oil and has been a brake on tougher U.N. action. The Center for Strategic and International Studies is calling the summit modest in ambition. Don't expect tariff breakthroughs. The November trade truce is intact through this fall, but no new deal is on the table. Trump arrives in Beijing knowing the Iran file is still open.

ANDREW: One more national thread to flag. Friday's April jobs report came in at 115,000 nonfarm payrolls, well above the 55,000 economists had penciled in. Unemployment held at 4.3 percent. Health care added 37,000 jobs, transportation and warehousing 30,000, retail 22,000. Federal government employment kept shrinking. Wage growth was a soft two tenths of a percent month over month. Bloomberg notes this is the first back-to-back monthly gain in nearly a year, but the trajectory is still well below late-2025 levels.

JENNY: That actually sets up the mortgage conversation, doesn't it.

ANDREW: It does, and that's where we're going next. The stronger jobs print pushed yields higher Friday, and we have the Warsh cloture vote tonight. The thirty-year fixed is sitting at 6.33 percent on Optimal Blue, 6.37 on Freddie Mac's weekly survey last Thursday. Two weeks ago that number was 6.30. So we've ticked back up modestly.

JENNY: And the Warsh vote tonight changes the calculus how?

ANDREW: Tonight at five thirty Eastern, the Senate votes on cloture for Kevin Warsh's nomination as Fed chair. Republicans hold 53 seats. Senator Fetterman has signaled he'd vote yes. The Banking Committee advanced him 13 to 11 on April 29th along strictly party lines, the first fully partisan committee vote on a Fed chair nominee in modern memory. Confirmation has to clear before Powell's term as chair expires Friday, May fifteenth. If Warsh gets through tonight, final confirmation likely lands midweek.

JENNY: And the bond market reaction, is that priced in already?

ANDREW: Mostly priced in, but not entirely. Warsh is viewed as marginally more hawkish than Powell on inflation and somewhat more open to letting market forces dictate rate paths. Mortgage strategists I read this weekend think a confirmed Warsh is worth roughly five to ten basis points of upward pressure on the ten-year, which translates into maybe a tenth of a point on the thirty-year fixed. The bigger swing factor for buyers is whether Warsh's first Fed policy meeting in June leans toward holding or signaling cuts. We'll know within four weeks of confirmation.

JENNY: So practically, if you're closing on a house this week?

ANDREW: If you have a rate lock that expires before Friday, you've probably ridden out the worst. If you're shopping fresh quotes, today and Tuesday are the noisier days. The Mortgage Bankers Association reported applications fell about four and a half percent last week, with the average purchase loan size hitting a record 467,300 dollars. That number tells you affordability is still the binding constraint, not rates moving a few basis points.

ANDREW: Jenny, over to you on the AI side.

JENNY: Thanks, Andrew. The big AI headline going into the weekend, and you flagged it, Anthropic is in talks to raise as much as 50 billion dollars at a valuation approaching 900 billion. The Financial Times broke the story Thursday, and TechCrunch confirmed multiple preemptive bids in the 850 to 900 billion range. If that closes, it would put Anthropic ahead of OpenAI's 852 billion post-money valuation from earlier this year.

ANDREW: What's driving that? Just enterprise momentum?

JENNY: Mostly the revenue numbers. Anthropic's annual run rate is now north of 30 billion dollars, possibly closer to 40, versus roughly 9 billion at the end of last year. That is fivefold revenue growth in less than 18 months, driven primarily by enterprise deals like the JPMorgan, Goldman, Citi, and Visa deployments, and the Microsoft 365 integration we covered last week. A board decision on whether to take the round is expected this month. And it may be Anthropic's final private raise before an I-P-O. Bloomberg is reporting that could come as early as October.

ANDREW: An October listing at near-trillion-dollar valuation would be one of the largest tech debuts ever.

JENNY: It would. For context, when OpenAI's 122 billion dollar round closed earlier this year, it was the largest private round in history. Anthropic is now negotiating a round bigger than that, and the lead bidders include some of the same names that just put a billion and a half dollars in via the Blackstone-Goldman joint venture two weeks ago. The capital concentration in two labs is becoming the story.

JENNY: Quick update on the government pre-release testing front. The Commerce Department's AI standards office confirmed last week that Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI have all signed on for pre-release model evaluation, joining OpenAI and Anthropic. Every major U.S. frontier lab is now in that program. First public test reports are expected this summer.

ANDREW: And on the deployment side, the Anthropic-F-I-S Financial Crimes agent we covered last week, that's the anti-money-laundering tool, now has named pilot banks. B-M-O and Amalgamated are first up, with a broader rollout penciled in for the back half of this year. The selling point is collapsing investigations from hours to minutes, and it's the first piece of the agent stack that has a clean compliance audit trail. Worth watching whether one of the top-four U.S. banks goes public on a deployment in the next 60 days.

JENNY: That's a real test of whether the enterprise momentum is durable or whether it's mostly headline announcements.

JENNY: Andrew, that's it from the AI desk. Let me bring this home to Jacksonville.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high near 86 degrees today, with patchy morning fog and an 80 percent chance of thunderstorms moving in after three in the afternoon. And here is the headline. That is real, organized rain for the first time in months. The cold front pushing through this afternoon is expected to drop up to an inch in spots, and the unsettled pattern lingers into Wednesday.

JENNY: That matters because Northeast Florida is still in exceptional drought. The rainfall deficit in Jacksonville proper is over 25 inches, and Florida had burn bans in 50 of 67 counties as of last week. Duval County's burn ban is still in effect this morning, and emergency officials are not lifting it on the first wet afternoon. But the fire risk profile changes meaningfully if Tuesday and Wednesday deliver.

ANDREW: How much rain would actually meaningfully dent the deficit, Jenny?

JENNY: Hydrologists at Jacksonville Today are saying it would take six to eight inches of sustained rainfall over the next several weeks to materially change the drought designation, not just one front. So today's storms are a real start, but anyone hoping the burn ban lifts this week is going to be disappointed. The more practical near-term win is air quality. Wildfire smoke from out-of-county fires has been bumping particulate readings into the moderate range for sensitive groups, and a steady rain will scrub that out by Wednesday.

ANDREW: Jenny, big local council vote coming up tomorrow, right?

JENNY: Yes, and this is the one to watch locally. The Jacksonville City Council takes up Ordinance 2026-326 tomorrow night, the 12 million dollar incentive package for Winn-Dixie's headquarters. The Finance Committee deferred it on May fifth over the company's plan to close the Harveys Supermarket on West Forty-Eighth Street in Brentwood. Councilmember Pittman has been working with the company on language that would tie the incentive to keeping that store open beyond an 18-month relocation window. The Jax Daily Record is reporting Winn-Dixie has agreed to that condition verbally, but the question tomorrow is whether the open-store stipulation lands in the actual ordinance text or stays a handshake.

ANDREW: Stakes if it fails?

JENNY: 200 jobs at roughly 100,000 dollars average. A 65 million dollar capital expenditure tied to the headquarters site. And, depending on how Winn-Dixie's German parent company reads the signal, a question about whether Jacksonville stays competitive for the next mid-sized corporate relocation. First Coast News is also reporting that the parent company's role in the deal is getting more scrutiny than it had two weeks ago. Council convenes at five in the evening tomorrow.

JENNY: One more local note. The Jaguars wrapped rookie minicamp Saturday at the Miller Electric Center. Coverage out of News4Jax suggests the offensive coaches are leaning into multi-tight-end sets this year, a response to last season's explosive plays allowed against Seattle and the Rams. The receiver room is still Travis Hunter, Brian Thomas Jr., Jakobi Meyers, and Parker Washington. No major roster surprises out of the weekend.

ANDREW: And the stadium piece in the background?

JENNY: Still on track for the 2026 home slate. EverBank capacity sits at 42,507 with the 400 Level upper bowl offline for construction. Eight games at EverBank, two in London back-to-back, and the Pittsburgh game in Orlando at Camping World. Season ticket retention numbers will start to leak this month, and that is the real economic indicator on whether the Stadium of the Future bet is holding the fan base.

JENNY: Andrew, the close is yours.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today. The Senate cloture vote on Kevin Warsh at five thirty Eastern. Three reasons it matters beyond the headline. One, if any Republican defects, the bond market will reprice the entire summer rate path Tuesday morning, and your mortgage quote will move. Two, Warsh's first Fed policy meeting is in June, and his confirmation tone tonight will telegraph whether he sees the April jobs print as reason to hold or reason to start cutting. Three, Powell's term expires Friday, and there is no plan B if cloture fails. Watch the Senate floor at five thirty. The market will be watching too.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Monday. Have a great start to the week.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Hormuz Skirmish Tests Ceasefire as Jobs Day Tests the Fed Path
item.description: U.S. and Iranian forces traded fire in the Strait of Hormuz Thursday as Tehran weighs a one-page peace memo. Plus April jobs day, mortgage rates ease, and Anthropic's Wall Street push deepens with FIS.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Friday, May 8th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: The April jobs report drops at 8:30 Eastern this morning, and Wall Street is bracing for what could be the slowest hiring number in nearly a year — about sixty-five thousand net new jobs.

ANDREW: And U.S. and Iranian forces traded fire across the Strait of Hormuz yesterday, even as President Trump insists the ceasefire is holding and Tehran says it is still drafting a response to the peace memo on the table.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed Thursday at seven thousand three hundred sixty-five, up one and a half percent — another record. The Dow added six hundred twelve points to finish just shy of forty-nine thousand nine hundred eleven, a gain of one point two percent. The Nasdaq jumped two percent to twenty-five thousand eight hundred thirty-nine, its best session in two weeks. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at 4.34 percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking right around 6.34 percent according to Mortgage News Daily. Futures this morning are slightly higher as traders wait for the payrolls print.

ANDREW: Let's start with the strait. Yesterday morning, three U.S. Navy destroyers were transiting the Strait of Hormuz when, according to Central Command, Iranian forces launched missiles, drones, and fast boats at the ships. The U.S. responded by hitting what it called Iranian military facilities. Tehran says American strikes hit civilian areas on Qeshm Island and along the southern Iranian coast. President Trump publicly called the exchange — quote — just a love tap, and insisted the ceasefire is still in effect.

JENNY: Wait — they're shooting at each other and the ceasefire is still on?

ANDREW: That is the position the White House is taking. The bigger story underneath the skirmish is that one-page memo we have been tracking all week. Fourteen points, brokered by Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner through Pakistani mediators. It would formally end the war and open a thirty-day window to negotiate Hormuz access, sanctions relief, and limits on Iran's nuclear program. Iran's foreign ministry said yesterday Tehran is still reviewing the proposal and — quote — has not reached a conclusion. Trump warned that without a deal, strikes will resume at a, quote, much higher level and intensity.

JENNY: So what does the market actually think happens?

ANDREW: Crude is down, equities are at records, and that tells you traders are pricing in a deal. If Iran rejects, expect both of those moves to reverse fast.

ANDREW: Now to this morning's other big story. At 8:30 Eastern, the Bureau of Labor Statistics releases the April jobs report. The consensus on Wall Street is roughly sixty-five thousand net new jobs — that would be the slowest hiring pace since last summer — with the unemployment rate holding at 4.3 percent. ADP's private-sector read on Tuesday came in at one hundred nine thousand, slightly above expectations, which has some economists nudging their headline number higher.

JENNY: And what does that do to the Fed picture?

ANDREW: It complicates it. A weak print pulls the rate-cut conversation back into the market. A strong print, combined with sticky energy prices from the Iran standoff, gives Kevin Warsh a more hawkish opening hand if he is confirmed next week. And we will come back to that confirmation vote in the close.

ANDREW: Jenny, that ten-year yield I mentioned at the top is exactly where the home-lending story picks up this morning.

JENNY: Take it away.

ANDREW: Mortgage rates are sitting right around 6.34 percent on the thirty-year fixed this morning, per Mortgage News Daily. That is down a few basis points from yesterday and well off the highs we hit two weeks ago when the Hormuz crisis first spiked crude oil. Freddie Mac's weekly PMMS print — the more conservative benchmark — is at 6.30 percent. We get a fresh PMMS at noon Eastern.

JENNY: Did the recent rate spike actually slow people down out there?

ANDREW: It did. The Mortgage Bankers Association reported Wednesday that applications fell four point four percent for the week ending May 1st. Purchase applications down about four percent, refinances down five. The MBA's tracked rate jumped to 6.45 from 6.37 the prior week — that is the spike showing up in the data. Here is the interesting wrinkle, though. The average purchase loan size hit a record four hundred sixty-seven thousand three hundred dollars. The buyers still active right now are buying expensive homes.

JENNY: So the affordability squeeze is selecting for high-end buyers.

ANDREW: Exactly. The bottom of the market is sitting on the sidelines waiting for rates to come back down. And how fast that happens depends almost entirely on what Iran does in the next week.

ANDREW: One more on home lending. FIS — the big bank technology vendor that runs the back office for thousands of banks and credit unions — announced this week a partnership with Anthropic to bring agentic AI directly into bank operations. The first product is a Financial Crimes agent that compresses anti-money-laundering investigations from hours down to minutes. BMO and Amalgamated Bank are the first named deployments, with broader availability planned for the second half of the year. Jenny, that is actually the perfect handoff into your AI beat.

JENNY: Thanks, Andrew. The FIS deal is the second half of an announcement Anthropic made Tuesday at a financial services briefing in New York. The first half — and the bigger story — is that Anthropic is now positioning itself as the operating layer for Wall Street. Claude is already in production at JPMorgan Chase, Goldman Sachs, Citi, AIG, and Visa, according to the company. And this week Anthropic released ten pre-built agent templates aimed at the largest banks.

ANDREW: Ten templates — what kinds of jobs are they automating?

JENNY: The headline one is a Know-Your-Customer screener. Others handle earnings analysis, investment memos, and the kind of cross-document research that used to occupy a junior analyst for half a day. They also rolled out full Microsoft 365 integration, so Claude can move context across Excel, PowerPoint, Word, and Outlook in a single agent — which sounds small but is a big deal for how analysts actually work.

ANDREW: And there's a new model attached to all of this?

JENNY: Yes — Claude Opus 4.7, their most capable model for financial work. The whole package sits on top of a one and a half billion dollar joint venture Anthropic announced last week with Blackstone, Hellman and Friedman, and Goldman to embed Claude across mid-market companies. Plus the two hundred billion dollar compute deal with Google Cloud we covered last week. Anthropic's annualized revenue is now approaching nineteen billion dollars, according to Bloomberg.

ANDREW: One thing I am watching: that is all enterprise. What about government?

JENNY: Mixed news there. The Commerce Department's Center for AI Standards and Innovation announced this week that Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI will join OpenAI and Anthropic in giving the government early access to their models for pre-release safety testing. So every major U.S. frontier lab is now in the program. But Anthropic is still locked out of the Pentagon's classified-network AI awards from May 1st, and the company has two federal lawsuits pending to overturn that designation.

ANDREW: So the commercial side is on fire and the defense side is still in court.

JENNY: That is the picture.

ANDREW: Jenny, what is happening at home?

JENNY: Weather first. Jacksonville is looking at a high near eighty-five degrees today with mostly sunny skies, light winds, and continued dry conditions. We are still in the worst drought period on record going back to 1872. The countywide burn ban remains in effect, and air quality has been elevated all week from wildfire smoke drifting in from surrounding counties.

ANDREW: Any sign of relief in the forecast?

JENNY: Not for at least the next several days. Forecasters are watching for any organized rainfall, but for now the Railroad Fire mop-up continues, and any new fire start in these conditions would be serious. The Red Flag Warning we covered yesterday has expired, but the underlying conditions have not changed.

JENNY: To local government. The Winn-Dixie incentive package is back on the agenda Tuesday night. As we have covered all week, the City Council Finance Committee deferred the twelve million dollar deal on May 5th after members objected to the company's plan to close the Harveys store at 201 West 48th Street near Brentwood. Since then, Winn-Dixie told Councilmember Ju'Coby Pittman the company will agree to keep that store open as a condition of the incentive package. That is per the Jacksonville Daily Record. The full Council takes it up Tuesday, May 12th.

ANDREW: Does the open-store stipulation actually make it into the ordinance language, or is it a handshake?

JENNY: That is the question Tuesday night. A verbal commitment from a corporate rep does not bind the company once the incentives are paid out. If council members want enforceability, the language has to be written into the ordinance. The deal itself is five and a half million dollars in a Recapture Enhanced Value Grant and six and a half million in a Headquarters Retention Grant, in exchange for Winn-Dixie keeping the Edgewood Court headquarters, two hundred jobs averaging about a hundred thousand dollars, and sixty-five million dollars in capital spending.

JENNY: One quick Jaguars note before we go. The team confirmed this week that EverBank Stadium will run at forty-two thousand five hundred seven seats for the 2026 season, with most of the upper bowl offline for stadium-of-the-future construction. Eight home games in Jacksonville, two in London, and the previously announced Pittsburgh game will play in Orlando at Camping World rather than London. Jacksonville blocked Pittsburgh from being scheduled as the London opponent.

ANDREW: Smart move. They keep that Pittsburgh gate inside Florida.

JENNY: Andrew, take us out.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this week — Monday afternoon, 5:30 Eastern. That is when the Senate holds the cloture vote on Kevin Warsh's nomination as Federal Reserve chair. Republicans hold fifty-three seats, Senator John Fetterman has signaled he will cross over and vote yes, and the Banking Committee advanced Warsh thirteen to eleven on April 29th — the first fully partisan committee vote on a Fed chair in history. Confirmation has to be done before Jay Powell's term ends May 15th. Watch for any Republican senator signaling unease over the weekend, and watch the bond market's reaction Tuesday morning. A clean confirmation likely steepens the yield curve on bets that Warsh's first FOMC meeting leans hawkish on inflation.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Friday. Have a great weekend.

ANDREW: We'll see you Monday.

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — Iran's Answer Due Today, Records on Wall Street, Winn-Dixie Saves a Store
item.description: Iran is expected to respond to a one-page U.S. peace memo today as markets close at fresh records. Plus, mortgage rates ease, Anthropic pushes deeper into Wall Street, and Winn-Dixie agrees to save a Brentwood grocery store.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Thursday, May 7th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: Today is the day Iran is expected to give its answer on a one-page memo to end the war — and Wall Street has already priced in optimism, with the S and P 500 and the Nasdaq closing at fresh record highs.

ANDREW: And we'll get into Anthropic's full-court press into financial services — pre-built AI agents for the largest banks, even as the Pentagon keeps the company at arm's length.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed at 7,365, up 1.5 percent — another all-time high. The Dow added 612 points, up 1.2 percent, to close at 49,911. The Nasdaq jumped 2 percent to 25,839, also a record. The ten-year Treasury yield fell about 7 basis points to 4.34 percent as oil prices eased, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking near 6.5 percent according to Mortgage News Daily, slightly lower on the day. The driver across all of these moves is the same — investors betting the Iran war is closer to ending than it is to escalating.

ANDREW: Which brings us to the lead story. Jenny, the framework we've been tracking all week may get its answer in the next several hours.

JENNY: That's the one-page, fourteen-point memo, right? Walk me through where it stands this morning.

ANDREW: Right. According to Axios and CNN, Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner have been negotiating directly and through Pakistani mediators with Iranian officials on a memorandum of understanding that would do three things — declare an end to the war, kick off a thirty-day window for a detailed deal, and freeze the status quo while that talks. Iran is expected to convey its response today. As of last night, Tehran's foreign ministry said the proposal was still under review, in part because every message has to travel to Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, who remains in hiding.

JENNY: So if Iran says yes, what actually changes between today and the next thirty days?

ANDREW: In the immediate term, the Strait of Hormuz reopens to commercial traffic, the U.S. Navy stops escorting tankers through, and Iran commits to a moratorium on nuclear enrichment while talks continue. In exchange, the U.S. would begin lifting sanctions and releasing frozen Iranian funds. The hard part — the part that's killed every previous deal — is what permanent limits on Iran's nuclear program look like. The MOU defers that to the thirty-day window, which is either a clever way to lock in a ceasefire or a way to paper over a disagreement that will explode in June.

JENNY: And President Trump has been saying both things this week.

ANDREW: He has. He's threatened intense bombing if a deal isn't reached, and at the same time told reporters he's optimistic. That's the public posture. The private posture, according to the Washington Post, is that the White House thinks Iran's leadership is genuinely divided and may not be able to forge consensus across factions even if Khamenei wants to.

JENNY: Andrew, separate from Iran — the Senate vote on Kevin Warsh is now four days out. Anything new on cloture?

ANDREW: Not much movement. The cloture vote is still scheduled for Monday at 5:30 in the afternoon. Republicans hold 53 seats. Senator Fetterman has signaled he'll vote yes, which gives the White House a small cushion. The full confirmation has to happen before Powell's term expires May 15th, so the timeline is tight but workable. We'll cover Warsh's first FOMC implications closer to the vote.

JENNY: One more national note before we move on — weekly jobless claims drop at 8:30 Eastern this morning. Last week's print was 189,000, the lowest reading since 1969. So the labor market is still telling us something very different from what the inflation data is telling us.

ANDREW: Which is exactly why the Fed's job gets harder by the week. A strong labor market argues against cutting, and an oil-driven inflation spike argues against cutting, but if Iran de-escalates this week, both of those pressures ease at once. That's the sequencing the bond market is trying to price right now.

JENNY: Speaking of rates, let's go to mortgages.
