ANDREW: The thirty-year fixed is tracking near 6.5 percent this morning according to Mortgage News Daily, with bond markets calmer after the Iran pullback in oil prices. That's down from a recent peak just under 6.6 percent and consistent with where rates sat before the Hormuz escalation. The Mortgage Bankers Association reported yesterday that overall application volume fell 4.4 percent for the week ending May 1, with purchase applications down 4 percent and refinances down 5.

JENNY: So buyers pulled back when rates spiked. Is the average loan size still climbing?

ANDREW: It is, and that's the data point worth noting. The MBA said the average purchase loan size hit $467,300 last week — the highest in the survey's history, which goes back to 1990. So the people who are still applying are buying more expensive homes. First-time and lower-income buyers are getting priced out, and the market is increasingly skewed toward move-up and equity-rich purchasers.

JENNY: That's a slow-motion problem for housing affordability — and for builders banking on entry-level demand.

ANDREW: It is. One more data point on the calendar — Freddie Mac's weekly survey hits at noon Eastern today. Last week the PMMS came in at 6.30 percent, up from 6.23 the week before. If today's print holds in the low sixes, that's broadly stable. If it pops above 6.4, that's the spillover from the Hormuz spike showing up in the weekly data.

JENNY: Got it. Andrew, let's move over to AI — and the company everyone's been talking about this week is Anthropic.

JENNY: On Tuesday in New York, Anthropic put on a financial services briefing and rolled out something specific — a suite of pre-built Claude agents aimed at the world's largest banks. Per Fortune, they also announced a $1.5 billion joint venture with Blackstone, Hellman and Friedman, and Goldman Sachs to embed those agents inside mid-sized financial firms. And the same day, they launched Claude Opus 4.7, with what Anthropic calls particular gains on the most difficult software engineering tasks.

ANDREW: That's a lot of distribution muscle for a company that, three weeks ago, was getting frozen out of Pentagon contracts.

JENNY: Right, and that contrast is the story. On May 1st, the Defense Department announced classified-network AI deals with eight vendors — Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Nvidia, OpenAI, SpaceX, Reflection AI, and one other — and Anthropic was specifically excluded under a supply chain risk designation. Per CNBC and DefenseScoop, Anthropic refused to allow Pentagon use of Claude for, quote, all lawful purposes, citing concerns about mass surveillance and autonomous weapons. They've sued the Trump administration in two separate federal courts to overturn the designation.

ANDREW: So Anthropic is making a bet — defense work isn't worth compromising their use policies, but financial services is the lane where they can grow. Is that the read?

JENNY: That's exactly the read. They're walking away from one of the most lucrative customers in the world and doubling down on banks, where they don't have the same policy disagreements. And the $200 billion compute deal with Google Cloud we covered Tuesday gives them the capacity to actually serve those banks. Anthropic also announced a full Microsoft 365 integration and a Moody's data partnership the same day, which is the kind of plumbing work that turns a model into a product banks can actually deploy.

ANDREW: That's the difference between a research lab and a vendor — and Anthropic is making clear which one they want to be. What about the regulatory backdrop?

JENNY: That tightened this week too. The Commerce Department's new Center for AI Standards and Innovation announced on Tuesday that Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and Elon Musk's xAI will all give the U.S. government early access to evaluate their models before public release. OpenAI and Anthropic were already doing this. So now the entire frontier — every major American lab — has agreed to pre-release government testing.

ANDREW: That's a significant shift from where this conversation was a year ago.

JENNY: It is. Andrew, before we head local — we're keeping an eye on the AI Standards Center over the next few weeks. The first public test reports are expected this summer, and they could either validate the labs' safety claims or surface things the public has never seen.

JENNY: Speaking of local — let's go to Jacksonville.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at a high near 96 degrees today with mostly sunny skies and gusty winds in the 20s and 30s. The National Weather Service has issued a Red Flag Warning for Duval County. Combined with the city's countywide burn ban, that means critical fire weather conditions today — please don't burn anything outside, and be careful with anything that throws a spark.

JENNY: Now to the news. The Winn-Dixie incentive package we've been tracking took a big step forward yesterday. After the Finance Committee deferred the deal Monday over concerns about a planned grocery store closure, the company has now agreed to keep that store open. Action News Jax and the Jacksonville Daily Record are reporting that Winn-Dixie told Council member Ju'Coby Pittman they'll preserve the Harveys Supermarket on West 48th Street as part of the deal — that's the Brentwood-area store that the city worried would create a federal food desert.

ANDREW: So the $12 million package is back on track?

JENNY: It looks that way. The package — $5.5 million in a Recapture Enhanced Value Grant and $6.5 million in a Headquarters Retention Grant — would keep Winn-Dixie's headquarters at Edgewood Court in West Jacksonville, with 200 new jobs at roughly $100,000 average pay and a $65 million capital investment. Finance Committee was clear — keep the Harveys open and they'll vote yes. The full Council is expected to take this up Tuesday at the May 12th meeting.

ANDREW: That's a meaningful win for the West 48th Street neighborhood — losing the only nearby grocery store would have been a much harder problem to solve later.

JENNY: It really would have. One other Jacksonville note — the Jaguars confirmed this week they will not host the Pittsburgh Steelers in London this year. Per reporting from Yardbarker and the team, Jacksonville used its ability to block Pittsburgh from being scheduled as a London opponent, which means the Steelers will play in Florida instead — at Camping World Stadium in Orlando, since EverBank is in reduced capacity for the Stadium of the Future renovation.

ANDREW: That's a small but interesting business decision — the team is choosing to keep the higher-profile home games on the Florida calendar even with reduced seating.

JENNY: Right. Eight home games at EverBank in 2026, two in London, upper deck closed, capacity at 42,500. We'll keep tracking.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch today — Iran's response to the one-page memo, expected through Pakistani mediators sometime in the next several hours. If Tehran relays a yes, even a conditional yes, expect oil to fall further, the ten-year yield to drop another leg, and mortgage rates to ease before Friday's close. If Iran punts or rejects the framework, the script flips fast — and President Trump's bombing threats become the next headline rather than a negotiating posture. Watch your news app between mid-morning and early afternoon Eastern. That's when we expect to know which way this goes.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Thursday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.
